are an Australian [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") band from [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney,_New_South_Wales "Sydney, New South Wales"), [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), formed in late 1987. Originally an instrumental band, they became more popular when fronted by vocalist [Tex Perkins](/wiki/Tex_Perkins "Tex Perkins") (also [Beasts of Bourbon](/wiki/Beasts_of_Bourbon "Beasts of Bourbon")). For the majority of the band's tenure, the band has featured Jim Elliott on drums, Ken Gormly on bass guitar, Dan Rumour on guitar and [James Cruickshank](/wiki/James_Cruickshank "James Cruickshank") on guitar and keyboards. Between 1990 and 2001, the band released six studio albums – three of which (1991's *[This Is Not the Way Home](/wiki/This_Is_Not_the_Way_Home "This Is Not the Way Home")*, 1993's *[The Honeymoon Is Over](/wiki/The_Honeymoon_Is_Over "The Honeymoon Is Over")* and 1995's *[Three Legged Dog](/wiki/Three_Legged_Dog "Three Legged Dog")*) were certified platinum by [ARIA](/wiki/Australian_Recording_Industry_Association "Australian Recording Industry Association"). The band are also known for their music having featured on television, such as "[Better Get a Lawyer](/wiki/Better_Get_a_Lawyer "Better Get a Lawyer")" soundtracking an [RTA](/wiki/Roads_%26_Traffic_Authority "Roads & Traffic Authority") ad targeting [drink driving](/wiki/Drink_driving "Drink driving"), and "Reckless Eyeballin'" – an instrumental track from their debut album *[Down Below](/wiki/Down_Below_(The_Cruel_Sea_album) "Down Below (The Cruel Sea album)")* – becoming the theme of Australian TV police drama *[Blue Heelers](/wiki/Blue_Heelers "Blue Heelers")*. The band has won eight [ARIA Music Awards](/wiki/ARIA_Music_Awards "ARIA Music Awards"), including five in [1994](/wiki/ARIA_Music_Awards_of_1994 "ARIA Music Awards of 1994") for work associated with *The Honeymoon Is Over*.

For other uses, see [The Cruel Sea](/wiki/The_Cruel_Sea_(disambiguation) "The Cruel Sea (disambiguation)").

**The Cruel Sea** are an Australian [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") band from [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney,_New_South_Wales "Sydney, New South Wales"), [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), formed in late 1987. Originally an instrumental band, they became more popular when fronted by vocalist [Tex Perkins](/wiki/Tex_Perkins "Tex Perkins") (also [Beasts of Bourbon](/wiki/Beasts_of_Bourbon "Beasts of Bourbon")). For the majority of the band's tenure, the band has featured Jim Elliott on drums, Ken Gormly on bass guitar, Dan Rumour on guitar and [James Cruickshank](/wiki/James_Cruickshank "James Cruickshank") on guitar and keyboards. Between 1990 and 2001, the band released six studio albums – three of which (1991's *[This Is Not the Way Home](/wiki/This_Is_Not_the_Way_Home "This Is Not the Way Home")*, 1993's *[The Honeymoon Is Over](/wiki/The_Honeymoon_Is_Over "The Honeymoon Is Over")* and 1995's *[Three Legged Dog](/wiki/Three_Legged_Dog "Three Legged Dog")*) were certified platinum by [ARIA](/wiki/Australian_Recording_Industry_Association "Australian Recording Industry Association"). The band are also known for their music having featured on television, such as "[Better Get a Lawyer](/wiki/Better_Get_a_Lawyer "Better Get a Lawyer")" soundtracking an [RTA](/wiki/Roads_%26_Traffic_Authority "Roads & Traffic Authority") ad targeting [drink driving](/wiki/Drink_driving "Drink driving"), and "Reckless Eyeballin'" – an instrumental track from their debut album *[Down Below](/wiki/Down_Below_(The_Cruel_Sea_album) "Down Below (The Cruel Sea album)")* – becoming the theme of Australian TV police drama *[Blue Heelers](/wiki/Blue_Heelers "Blue Heelers")*. The band has won eight [ARIA Music Awards](/wiki/ARIA_Music_Awards "ARIA Music Awards"), including five in [1994](/wiki/ARIA_Music_Awards_of_1994 "ARIA Music Awards of 1994") for work associated with *The Honeymoon Is Over*.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Cruel_Sea_(band)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Formation and early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Cruel_Sea_(band)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation and early years")]

Danny Rumour (aka Daniel John Atkins)[[1]](#cite_note-APRASly-1) was a member of [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") bands Blackrunner, Urban Guerrillas, Friction, Ugly Mirrors and Bedhogs in Sydney from the mid-1970s to 1980.[[2]](#cite_note-ARDb-2) In 1980 he formed Sekret Sekret which played a "sprightly brand of punky power pop with psychedelic overtones".[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3) Sekret Sekret would often play at Sydney pub, [The Grand Hotel](/wiki/Grand_Hotel_%E2%80%93_Broadway "Grand Hotel – Broadway"),[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:The_Cruel_Sea_(band)#Dubious "Talk:The Cruel Sea (band)")*] with Rumour assembling an *ad hoc* line-up of musicians using instruments housed at the venue.[[4]](#cite_note-Howl-4) With lead vocalist [David Virgin](/wiki/David_Virgin "David Virgin") (ex-Ugly Mirrors), Des Devlin (ex-Ugly Mirrors) and Peter Mullany (ex-[Johnny Dole & The Scabs](/wiki/Johnny_Dole_%26_The_Scabs "Johnny Dole & The Scabs")) they released four independent singles by 1984. When they broke up in 1984, the line-up was Rumour (guitar), [Virgin](/wiki/David_Virgin "David Virgin") (vocals), Devlin (guitar), James Elliot (drums) and Ken Gormly (bass guitar).

After Sekret Sekret disbanded, The Cruel Sea was formed in late 1987 by Elliot on drums and Rumour on guitar. They enlisted Dee Corben on bass guitar and his brother, Gerard "Ged" Corben (also in [Lime Spiders](/wiki/Lime_Spiders "Lime Spiders")), on guitar.[[2]](#cite_note-ARDb-2) The name was from a 1964 surf instrumental, "[Cruel Sea](/wiki/Walk,_Don%27t_Run_%2764 "Walk, Don't Run '64")", by United States group [The Ventures](/wiki/The_Ventures "The Ventures") (a cover of [The Dakotas'](/wiki/The_Dakotas_(band) "The Dakotas (band)") 1963 single, which was in turn inspired by the [novel](/wiki/The_Cruel_Sea_(novel) "The Cruel Sea (novel)") and [film](/wiki/The_Cruel_Sea_(1953_film) "The Cruel Sea (1953 film)") of the same name).[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3) Early gigs in 1988 were played at the Harold Park Hotel, behind a pool table where space was so tight that the guitarists had to move out of the way when pool players took a shot.[[4]](#cite_note-Howl-4) The original line-up played about 20 shows and parties, then Dee Corben left, he was replaced by former Sekret Sekret bandmate, Gormly.[[2]](#cite_note-ARDb-2) Barry Turnbull (ex-[John Kennedy's Love Gone Wrong](/wiki/John_Kennedy_(Australian_musician)#John_Kennedy.27s_Lov_Gone_Wrong "John Kennedy (Australian musician)"), [The Widdershins](/wiki/The_Widdershins "The Widdershins")) briefly substituted for Gormly on bass guitar. James Cruickshank (The Widdershins) joined on keyboards and guitars.[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Howl-4)

### Early albums: *Down Below* and *This Is Not the Way Home*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Cruel_Sea_(band)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early albums: Down Below and This Is Not the Way Home")]

Main articles: [Down Below (The Cruel Sea album)](/wiki/Down_Below_(The_Cruel_Sea_album) "Down Below (The Cruel Sea album)") and [This Is Not the Way Home](/wiki/This_Is_Not_the_Way_Home "This Is Not the Way Home")

In 1989, The Cruel Sea invited vocalist [Tex Perkins](/wiki/Tex_Perkins "Tex Perkins"), their lighting technician and member of [Beasts of Bourbon](/wiki/Beasts_of_Bourbon "Beasts of Bourbon"), to join them on-stage.[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Howl-4) Perkins had written lyrics for some of their instrumentals. The band was signed by [Red Eye Records](/wiki/Red_Eye_Records_(label) "Red Eye Records (label)") and released a 12" extended play (EP), *Down Below*, in September. It contained nine tracks and was produced by Phil Punch ([The Mexican Spitfires](/wiki/The_Mexican_Spitfires "The Mexican Spitfires")) and The Cruel Sea.[[2]](#cite_note-ARDb-2) It was followed by an eleven-track album of the same name, *[Down Below](/wiki/Down_Below_(The_Cruel_Sea_album) "Down Below (The Cruel Sea album)")* in December 1990; both releases featured Perkins on vocals. "Reckless Eyeballin'" – an instrumental track on the album – later became the theme song of Australian TV police drama, *[Blue Heelers](/wiki/Blue_Heelers "Blue Heelers")* (1994–2006). Although Perkins was also performing with Beasts of Bourbon, The Cruel Sea built a following on the inner-city [pub rock](/wiki/Pub_rock_(Australia) "Pub rock (Australia)") circuit with "atmospheric music [that] evoked the feel of wide open spaces".[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3) During 1990, Ged Corben left to focus on his work with Lime Spiders.

A single, "I Feel" was released in September 1991 ahead of their second album, *[This Is Not the Way Home](/wiki/This_Is_Not_the_Way_Home "This Is Not the Way Home")* issued in October.[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3) It was produced by [Tony Cohen](/wiki/Tony_Cohen "Tony Cohen") ([The Birthday Party](/wiki/The_Birthday_Party_(band) "The Birthday Party (band)"), Beasts of Bourbon) and The Cruel Sea.[[2]](#cite_note-ARDb-2) The album provided a range of music "from funky Louisiana swamp blues to sweet soul".[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3) Vocals by Perkins were compared with [Captain Beefheart](/wiki/Captain_Beefheart "Captain Beefheart"), [John Lee Hooker](/wiki/John_Lee_Hooker "John Lee Hooker") and [Tony Joe White](/wiki/Tony_Joe_White "Tony Joe White").[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3) "4" followed in March 1992 with "This Is Not the Way Home" released as a single in August.[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3) After the album's release, the band toured Europe in support of [Nick Cave and the Bad Seeds](/wiki/Nick_Cave_and_the_Bad_Seeds "Nick Cave and the Bad Seeds").[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3) In March 1993[[5]](#cite_note-5) they released, "[Black Stick](/wiki/Black_Stick "Black Stick")" as a single, which peaked at No. 25 on the [Australian Recording Industry Association](/wiki/Australian_Recording_Industry_Association "Australian Recording Industry Association") (ARIA) [Singles Chart](/wiki/ARIA_Charts "ARIA Charts").[[6]](#cite_note-aus-6) *[This Is Not the Way Home](/wiki/This_Is_Not_the_Way_Home "This Is Not the Way Home")* received a nomination for 'Best Group' at the [ARIA Music Awards of 1993](/wiki/ARIA_Music_Awards_of_1993 "ARIA Music Awards of 1993").[[7]](#cite_note-ARIAList-7)

### Mainstream success: *The Honeymoon Is Over* and *Three Legged Dog*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Cruel_Sea_(band)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Mainstream success: The Honeymoon Is Over and Three Legged Dog")]

Main articles: [The Honeymoon Is Over](/wiki/The_Honeymoon_Is_Over "The Honeymoon Is Over") and [Three Legged Dog](/wiki/Three_Legged_Dog "Three Legged Dog")

In early 1993, The Cruel Sea had Perkins on-board full-time with his commitment to Beasts of Bourbon on hold. The Cruel Sea's third album, *[The Honeymoon Is Over](/wiki/The_Honeymoon_Is_Over "The Honeymoon Is Over")* was produced by the band, Cohen and [Mick Harvey](/wiki/Mick_Harvey "Mick Harvey") ([Robert Forster](/wiki/Robert_Forster_(musician) "Robert Forster (musician)"), [Anita Lane](/wiki/Anita_Lane "Anita Lane")) of Nick Cave and the Bad Seeds. Released in May, it peaked at No. 4 on the ARIA Albums Chart, and sold over 140,000 copies.[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3)[[6]](#cite_note-aus-6) The title song, "The Honeymoon Is Over" reached the Top 50 in August.[[6]](#cite_note-aus-6) It was followed by a cover of White's 1969 song, "Woman with Soul", which peaked at No. 64.[[6]](#cite_note-aus-6) Perkins also performed as a member in the country-blues trio, [Tex, Don and Charlie](/wiki/Tex,_Don_and_Charlie "Tex, Don and Charlie") and released *[Sad But True](/wiki/Sad_But_True_(Tex,_Don_and_Charlie_album) "Sad But True (Tex, Don and Charlie album)")* (1993).

At the [ARIA Music Awards of 1994](/wiki/ARIA_Music_Awards_of_1994 "ARIA Music Awards of 1994"), The Cruel Sea won 'Single of the Year' and 'Song of the Year' for "The Honeymoon is Over", 'Album of the Year' and 'Best Group' for *The Honeymoon is Over* and received three further nominations including 'Best Cover Art' by Kristyna Higgins and Jay Manby.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[7]](#cite_note-ARIAList-7)[[9]](#cite_note-ARIA1994-9) At an after-party, a drunken guest attacked Higgins, a professional photographer, and a fracas ensued with Perkins defending his partner. Also that night, two of their ARIA trophies were stolen.[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3) Late in 1994, the group toured Europe, again supporting Nick Cave and the Bad Seeds. They followed with a headlining tour across Europe and to Canada.[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3)

The next album, *[Three Legged Dog](/wiki/Three_Legged_Dog "Three Legged Dog")*, was produced by Cohen, the group and Paul McKercher ([Clouds](/wiki/Clouds_(Australian_band) "Clouds (Australian band)")).[[2]](#cite_note-ARDb-2) It was released in April 1995 and peaked at No. 1.[[4]](#cite_note-Howl-4)[[6]](#cite_note-aus-6) The Cruel Sea toured Canada, United States and Europe, followed by a support slot for [The Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones") on the Australian leg of their [Voodoo Lounge Tour](/wiki/Voodoo_Lounge_Tour "Voodoo Lounge Tour").[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3) Top 50 singles from *[Three Legged Dog](/wiki/Three_Legged_Dog "Three Legged Dog")* were "[Better Get a Lawyer](/wiki/Better_Get_a_Lawyer "Better Get a Lawyer")" (November 1994), "[Just a Man](/wiki/Just_a_Man_(The_Cruel_Sea_song) "Just a Man (The Cruel Sea song)")" (March 1995) and "Anybody But You" (July).[[6]](#cite_note-aus-6) The album won an [ARIA in 1995](/wiki/ARIA_Music_Awards_of_1995 "ARIA Music Awards of 1995") for 'Best Group' and two nominations, 'Album of the Year', and 'Best Cover Art' for Higgins and Jim Paton.[[7]](#cite_note-ARIAList-7)[[10]](#cite_note-ARIA1995-10)

### Later years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Cruel_Sea_(band)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Later years")]

Main articles: [Over Easy](/wiki/Over_Easy "Over Easy") and [Where There's Smoke (The Cruel Sea album)](/wiki/Where_There%27s_Smoke_(The_Cruel_Sea_album) "Where There's Smoke (The Cruel Sea album)")

The Cruel Sea's next album, *[Rock'n Roll Duds](/wiki/Rock%27n_Roll_Duds "Rock'n Roll Duds")*, was a compilation of [b-sides](/wiki/B-side "B-side") and studio outtakes, released in November 1995.[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3) The group had a two-year hiatus in releases, Perkins issued his first solo album, *Far Be it from Me* (1996) and contributed to Beasts of Bourbon's *[Gone](/wiki/Gone_(Beasts_of_Bourbon_album) "Gone (Beasts of Bourbon album)")* (1997). The Cruel Sea returned to their instrumental roots and embarked on a series of gigs without Perkins including the [Big Day Out](/wiki/Big_Day_Out "Big Day Out") tour.[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3)

In February 1998, with Perkins returned, the group released a single, "Hard Times" ahead of its album *[Over Easy](/wiki/Over_Easy "Over Easy")* in August. The album was produced by Daniel Denholm ([Frente!](/wiki/Frente! "Frente!"), [Boom Crash Opera](/wiki/Boom_Crash_Opera "Boom Crash Opera")), [Phil McKellar](/wiki/Phil_McKellar "Phil McKellar") ([Grinspoon](/wiki/Grinspoon "Grinspoon"), [Frenzal Rhomb](/wiki/Frenzal_Rhomb "Frenzal Rhomb")), the band and McKercher.[[2]](#cite_note-ARDb-2)[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3) It peaked at No. 13 and was followed by their Takin All Day national tour through most of 1998.[[3]](#cite_note-McF-3)[[6]](#cite_note-aus-6) The band's first greatest hits album, *[The Most](/wiki/The_Most_(The_Cruel_Sea_album) "The Most (The Cruel Sea album)")* appeared in November 1999 and reached the Top 50. After the success of his first album, Perkins released his second solo album *Dark Horses* (2000).

In August 2001, [ABC TV](/wiki/ABC_TV_(Australian_TV_channel) "ABC TV (Australian TV channel)") broadcast the series, *[Long Way to the Top](/wiki/Long_Way_to_the_Top "Long Way to the Top")*.[[11]](#cite_note-ABCTVDoco-11) Perkins featured on "Episode 6: Gathering of the Tribes 1984–2000" where he discussed his non-mainstream work with both Beasts of Bourbon and The Cruel Sea, which were "Providing the poor forgotten 5% with something – who like the really fucked up weird shit".[[12]](#cite_note-LWEp6-12) In September their next album, *[Where There's Smoke](/wiki/Where_There%27s_Smoke_(The_Cruel_Sea_album) "Where There's Smoke (The Cruel Sea album)")*, produced by [Magoo](/wiki/Magoo_(Australian_producer) "Magoo (Australian producer)") ([Regurgitator](/wiki/Regurgitator "Regurgitator"), [Midnight Oil](/wiki/Midnight_Oil "Midnight Oil")) and the band,[[2]](#cite_note-ARDb-2) appeared and reached the Top 30.[[4]](#cite_note-Howl-4)[[6]](#cite_note-aus-6) It was followed by another compilation, *We Don't Work, We Play Music* in October 2002 with "Groovy Situation" issued as a single.[[4]](#cite_note-Howl-4)

Individual members concentrated on side or solo projects. In early 2005, Perkins returned to Tex Don and Charlie and released *All is Forgiven* in March. Guitarist and main composer, Rumour began touring and recording with his own roots-style instrumental band, the Dan Rumour Band, and Elliot joined on drums by mid-2006. In August, Perkins declared on [ABC2](/wiki/ABC2 "ABC2")'s *Dig* radio program that The Cruel Sea were no more. Dan Rumour Band appeared on the Australian surf music project *Delightful Rain* released October, and a documentary film of the same name on Australian television in December. Rumour's first solo album was released in October 2007 as by [Dan Rumour and The Drift](/wiki/Nick_Larkins "Nick Larkins").

In spite of his earlier statement, The Cruel Sea with Perkins toured Melbourne and Sydney in 2008 before their Blues & Roots Festival performances. They also toured Australia in 2010.[[13]](#cite_note-Access-13)[[14]](#cite_note-Pepper-14) The full band joined [Bernard Fanning](/wiki/Bernard_Fanning "Bernard Fanning") as support for his Day On The Green tour of Australia in October to November 2013.

Guitarist and keyboardist James Cruickshank died on 8 October 2015 after a long battle with bowel cancer.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 2023, the band reconvened to celebrate the 30th anniversary of the classic 1993 release, The Honeymoon Is Over, with the addition of longtime collaborator, guitarist Matt Walker. This led to recording sessions for a new album, Straight into the Sun.[[1]](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2024-10-04/the-cruel-sea-new-album-straight-out-sun-23-years-tex-perkins/104427368)

In 2026 the band joined [Paul Kelly](/wiki/Paul_Kelly_(Australian_musician) "Paul Kelly (Australian musician)"), [Missy Higgins](/wiki/Missy_Higgins "Missy Higgins"), [The Cat Empire](/wiki/The_Cat_Empire "The Cat Empire"), [Kasey Chambers](/wiki/Kasey_Chambers "Kasey Chambers") and [Jess Hitchcock](/w/index.php?title=Jess_Hitchcock&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jess Hitchcock (page does not exist)") in the *Red Hot Summer Tour* which touredprimarily regional centres in Australia.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Members

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Cruel_Sea_(band)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Members")]

**Current members**

* Jim Elliott – drums (1987–2003, 2008, 2010, 2023–present)
* Dan Rumour – guitar, clavinet (1987–2003, 2008, 2010, 2023–present)
* Ken Gormly – bass guitar (1988–1990, 1990–2003, 2008, 2010, 2023–present)
* [Tex Perkins](/wiki/Tex_Perkins "Tex Perkins") – lead vocals, harmonica, occasional guitar (1989–2003, 2008, 2010, 2023–present)
* [Matt Walker](/wiki/Matt_Walker_(Australian_musician) "Matt Walker (Australian musician)") – guitar, keyboards, backing vocals (2023–present)

**Former members**

* Dee Corben – bass guitar (1987–1988)
* Gerard Corben – guitar (1987–1990)
* Barry Turnbull – bass guitar (1990)
* [James Cruickshank](/wiki/James_Cruickshank "James Cruickshank") – keyboards, guitar, backing vocals (1988–2003, 2008, 2010; died 2015)
